sentences.text: I understand.
My name is Steve Lesniak, L-E-S-N-I-A-K.
My name when I was born was Goldberger, G-O-L-D-B-E-R-G-E-R.
Solomon.
No.
Was born the 28th of July, 1920.
I was born in the city of Krakow in the country Poland.
Today, I am 75 years old.
The city what I was born-- I'm talking about her later years-- in 1939, the population was approximately 300,000 people, including 63,000 Jews living in the city of Krakow.
The city of Krakow was, actually, they were calling it the City of the Kings, because in previous years, history is telling us, that all the kings, practically, were living in the city of Krakow.
Used to be like a fortress.
They call it Wawel-- W-A-W-E-L.
This is a place where many, many tourists used to come to visit the city.
And actually, whenever they came to visit, they always visit the fortress.
Yes.
There were a number of synagogues in the cities.
My family used to belong to one.
We used to pray at least a few times a year on those High Holy Days.
My family was very assimilated Jews, but still they were attending the synagogue regularly, like Saturdays or every holidays.
My home was a happy home.
We're a family of three.
In the beginning, my brother, Roman, who's still alive, was born in 1922.
He's two years younger.
But in 1933, we had another addition to our family.
Was born to me, a sister.
Her name was Halina.
Beautiful child, blonde with black eyes.
Our family was very much respecting each other.
We had a wonderful [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] knowledge about everything which was going on about us.
Our house was consisted of two rooms and a kitchen.
We had, like, one bedroom, where-- and one room was divided into a bedroom.
There were five people living in this house.
As long as I remember, we were always living in this house.
His name was Ignaszy Goldberger.
He was a metal worker.
He had a business on his own.
He had a few worker who was working with him.
He was a wonderful man as long I remember him.
He married very young, because after the war, immediately he married my mother-- his wife.
My mother--
This was the First World War, from 1914 to 1918.
After the war ended, my parents got married.
My mother was 17 years old; my father, 21.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were three kids, like I mentioned before, born to them.
My father used to be a very good friend of myself and my brother.
He was helping us any-- with anything he could.
Like, for instance, I remember when we were growing up, he organized for us a group of people who were playing soccer.
And he form a club, because he by himself was, when he was young, was a soccer player and a weightlifter.
And he was playing even-- I remember like today-- even when we were already teenagers, he was playing sometimes with us, soccer.
Tell me about your mother.
What was her name?
Her name was Genia-- G-E-N-I-A-- Goldberger, born Hillinger, H-I-double L-I-N-G-E-R.
She was a very nice person, quiet, very understanding.
And she-- she would do anything for her children and her husband.
Was a really beautiful family life.
Did your mother have any special interests or activities?
She used to like to go to concert, opera, or any plays.
This was her life.
And actually, movies, because those days we didn't have television yet.
Yes, she did.
Every Friday she was doing cooking and-- and preparing for the evening, for the Shabbat.
And not even that, but she was baking sometimes cakes, and delivering to the poor people in the building, too.
But this was happening practically every Friday.
Oh, actually, it was always a festivity in our home for preparation, because naturally, we saw it-- my mother was cooking, preparing.
The parents were trying, always, to buy new shoes for the kids, or a suit, or anything in order to look good.
And normally, we were going to the synagogue, spending the holidays, coming home, having lunch, and visiting our members of family.
Together, we were 63 family member in the city of Krakow.
And we were extremely close, because we were seeing each other practically on daily basis with many, many of our family.

Passover was a big festivity, because my late mother, she was preparing, always, food, all kinds of specialities, because this was always remembered by us as the days of eating, getting together with the family, and enjoying our life.
Yes, I did.
Purim was another holiday where my mother was preparing all kinds of baskets, sending to the family.
Their delivery, naturally, was by my brother, myself, for which I remember like today.
They were always tipping us with a few cent at the time.
And this was going on practically on yearly basis.
I beg your pardon?
I went to a public school for seven years.
And was not far where I used to live.
It took me approximately 10 to 12 minutes walking.
And I used to like very much the school, because there were many, many kids my age.
What we had many things in common.
We used to play soccer.
We used to learn together.
We used to play together.
And as long I remember, this was a very beautiful time in our life.
In this school there were only Jewish kids.
Yes, not-- but not much.
Only with the children living in our building or next buildings-- playing together soccer, or biking, or skiing in later years.
I was always aware of anti-Semitism.
I personally didn't have any problems.
But I heard and I saw quite a number of things what I didn't like it.
Even as a young child, it used to hurt me very much, because I knew we were unable to defend ourself.
I saw many times when they were throwing-- throwing stone against Jewish kids, or come over and beat them up-- sometimes badly.
And things like that were quite often.
Yes, I did.
They were always trying to keep me out of any problems.
And they were always talking to us, try to avoid any scene like that.
Just walk away.
Yes, I did.
I had a bar mitzvah in our synagogue.
And I remember like today.
I got a new bike, which was something very big, because today we don't think that way.
But to get a bike, a new bike, they used to call it at that time, [NON-ENGLISH].
But this was really something very, very strong for any young child who was 13 years old, to receive a new bike.
No.
Far from it.
It was a celebration just in the synagogue.
Like they called me to the Torah.
And I had to say some words and pray together with the older people.
And then after that, I remember my father donate at that time to what they call a kiddush, what is a festivity.
People were drinking and eating for a number of hours and singing.
No.
No.
No.
Yes.
We were getting the news.
We had a radio in our house.
And we were listening to the stories, what was happening in Germany.
When Hitler came to power in 1933, we were-- we knew about, practically on a daily basis, what was going on.
And we were scared.
Every one of us was scared, because we knew exactly what the man has on his mind.
And in 1936, after the Olympic in Berlin, at that time, people started to be already very nervous, because they knew what was coming.
And everybody was afraid something like that can happen in Poland, too.
And in 1938, when Hitler started to expelled the Jews, what their background was, actually, they were born in Poland.
That time we knew from the people who were coming to Krakow, to our city, what was happening, and how dangerous the situation in Germany.
And everybody was worried something like that can happen in Poland, too.
No.
I went for three years of business administration.
And after that, when I was 16, 17 years old, I was working for my uncle.
Selling material.
At that time, to get into my own business one day, because that's how my uncle was always, always preaching.
If you want to be successful, you have to be on your own.
This was my goal.
That's right.
Telling you the truth, at that time, it wasn't that easy.
First of all, to transfer our self where-- my father was making a good living in Poland.
We-- people were scared to make a move.
[AIRPLANE]
Naturally, the big break was in 1st of September, 1939, when Hitler attacked Poland.
And a few days later, tanks and the military, the German might was already in our city, Krakow.
By then, we knew exactly what to expect, because everybody was terribly scared.
We knew what was happening to other people.
We knew what happened Crystal Night in 1938.
And people didn't know how to react.
Everybody was talking.
But not too many people had a chance to get out from the country.
From people what I knew, no one got out from the city.
It was already too late.
Yes.
It started actually immediately after they took over.
They asked us to register everybody.
We were walking the city of Krakow quite freely.
But in no time, the military was grabbing any one of us from the street, throwing into trucks, and they were taking us to all kinds of works.
Most of the time, winter time, we used to break the ice on the street, clean the snow.
And always a group of Germans were watching over us, and kicking and beating.
They were doing to us anything they want to, because they had all the control.
Many from our people were trying to hide themself in order not to walk out.
They were entering our homes, picking up anybody who could work.
In 1940, we all had to register for work.
They form, like, a Jewish groups.
And each group was sent to a different working place.
This was going on till around 1941.
Naturally, they were coming to our home, picking up anything they want-- this was a [INAUDIBLE], jewelry-- beating up everybody.
It was a terrible time in our life.
No.
He was trying to do some work, but he couldn't because they were picking us up, including him, taking to all kinds of work.
And after certain hours, this was quite late-- could be 7, 8 o'clock-- they let us free to go home.
Because by then, we were still living in our house.
In 1941, beginning of '41 in the month of March, we were sent to a ghetto just over the Vistula River.
We had to cross the bridge.
Took us around one hour.
Yes.
They were talking already in the city, this one day, we'll have to leave our homes and they're building up a ghetto, like I mentioned before, just over the bridge over the Vistula River.
No.
Nobody was paid for the work, because didn't exist, such a thing.
Jews were used to the worst type of work, whatever exist at that time.
And no one of us got paid.
If we walked away without a beating, this was already a pay.
After a while, you feel like a beat-up dog.
You don't react.
You scared.
You follow every move or every word what they telling you to do.
We slowly, slowly starting to lose our will, our mind.
We're plain and simple like-- like a cow, without any brain whatsoever.
The food situation was always scary, because you couldn't buy-- if you want to buy something extra, you had to have big money.
We didn't have that much money.
What we were living was a ration-- that they were giving us a certain amount of bread, or milk, or whatever was coming with it.
But at the time, it was getting worse from day to day.
By that time, no.
We were still following the Jewish calendar.
But naturally everything was in our homes-- not officially.
Our private valuable, yes.
But furniture and what have you, we were unable to take it with us.
We had to leave everything in the house.
By then, they were coming already in our homes and taking out all the furniture, whatever they could put the hand on, because they knew exactly as we cannot touch anything.
So it was an ugly situation at that time.
We were terribly depressed.
And when we were walking toward the ghetto, they were lining up the streets.
And was a ugly scene.
They were throwing stones, spitting, and laughing, rejoicing.
Stays with me till today.
Was a ugly scene.
Lousy, bad.
Deprived of humanity.
Each and every one of us got from the committee a room.
We got two rooms, our family.
But immediately after that, we got another family who were living in our house, and another couple.
There were, together, 11 people staying in a room and a half.
A small kitchen, what [INAUDIBLE], yes.
It was the same situation.
We're getting rations, like certain amount of bread or whatever was coming with it.
But it was already very bad.
We're most of the time hungry.
Yes.
I was working in ghetto till 1942.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, yes.
Was a Judenrat.
And they were actually controlling each and everybody's life.
And the superiors over them were Germans.
They were telling them exactly what they want.
There were a number of times where they were expelling a few thousand Jews.
So a list came in that the Judenrat sent the Jewish policeman to each and every house, bringing a [? contingence, ?] whatever they ask.
Like, from a family they could take one or two people.
And this was going on for the first two years in ghetto-- from March 1941 till actually the liquidation of the ghetto, which was March 13, 1943.
There-- it was a hospital.
But naturally, they were lacking medication.
But we had a number of doctors who were still at the time in ghetto.
And we had the hospital, yes.
Privately at home, yes.
As long we were together, we could bear anything.
The tragedy started when they took my mother away.
This was in September, October 1942-- '42.
When we came back and we didn't find our mother, we knew right away she was sent away with a transport to a certain destination.
We didn't know where.
People were talking about different places, but nothing was proven, ever.
